title: AUDIT.json — Security tool output standard
description: Community standard for security-tool output. v0.1 proposal. Authored by Lictor AI, released CC0 — the spec belongs to nobody. Conforming tools emit this format alongside their native output so downstream consumers (dashboards, IDE plugins, AI translators, compliance exporters) can interop freely.

spec_version.description: Semver string declaring which version of the AUDIT.json spec this document conforms to. Consumers must check this before parsing — breaking changes go in v0.2 → v1.0, etc.

name.description: Lowercase identifier for the tool. Should be the canonical name (e.g., 'lictor', 'snyk', 'semgrep', 'trivy', 'aikido', 'github-code-scanning').

version.description: Semver string of the tool that produced this output. Used by consumers to handle quirks per tool-version.

vendor.description: Optional vendor / publisher name or domain. Helpful when the same tool name is used by multiple projects.

type.description: What kind of target was audited. 'repository' = local or remote source code. 'deployed-site' = a live URL (Shield-style). 'container-image' = Docker/OCI. 'package' = a single dependency tarball. 'binary' = a compiled artifact. 'iac' = Infrastructure as Code template.

url_or_path.description: Filesystem path, repository URL, or live site URL identifying the target.

platform_fingerprint.description: For AI-built apps: which build platform produced the target. Lets downstream tools apply platform-specific rules. Use 'custom' for manually written code; 'unknown' if you couldn't detect.

platform_confidence.description: Confidence (0.0–1.0) that platform_fingerprint is correct. Tools without a fingerprinting engine should omit this field rather than set 0.

commit_sha.description: For repository targets: the git commit SHA the audit ran against. Lets consumers reproduce the audit on the same code state.

branch.description: Optional git branch name for repository targets.

started.description: ISO 8601 timestamp with timezone offset for when the audit began.

completed.description: ISO 8601 timestamp with timezone offset for when the audit finished. Omit only for in-progress audits.

duration_ms.description: Wall-clock duration of the audit in milliseconds. Should reconcile with completed − started.

checks_run.description: Number of distinct checks the tool ran. Tools that don't have discrete checks (e.g., LLM-driven audits) can set this to 1 or omit.

partial.description: True if the audit was incomplete (e.g., timeout, error, scope-limited). Findings may be partial; consumers should warn the user.

summary.description: Counts of findings by severity. Must reconcile with findings.length.

total.description: Total count across all severities. MUST equal critical + high + medium + low + info AND MUST equal findings array length.

findings.description: The list of findings. May be empty (clean audit = empty array, not absent field).

id.description: Tool-scoped unique identifier for this finding. Format is tool-specific but should be stable across runs against the same code. Lictor uses 'L-YYYY-NNNN' format.

severity.description: 5-level severity ladder. 'critical' = immediate exploitation possible with real-world impact. 'high' = exploitable with effort, real impact. 'medium' = exploitable in specific conditions, moderate impact. 'low' = unlikely or low-impact. 'info' = informational only, no action needed.

category.description: Free-form category tag. A reference vocabulary will ship in vocabulary.json v0.2. Encouraged values: secrets, auth, database, rls, cors, prompt-injection, pii-leak, dependency, iac-misconfig, xss, csrf, idor, supply-chain, secret-in-llm-input, data-exfiltration, other.

title.description: Plain-English title for the finding. Should be a complete short sentence the user can read at a glance. Max 120 chars but aim for 80.

summary.description: 1–3 sentence plain-English description of the finding. Focus on impact ('anyone can read every user's row'), not jargon ('information disclosure vulnerability'). Lictor uses this as the report-body voice.

evidence.description: Where the finding lives in the audited target.

file_path.description: Relative path to the file containing the finding. Use forward slashes regardless of host OS.

line.description: 1-indexed line number where the finding is anchored. For multi-line findings, use the first relevant line.

line_end.description: Optional last line for multi-line findings.

code_snippet.description: The actual code or content at the finding location. Tools should redact secrets before including (e.g., show 'eyJ...' not the full JWT).

url.description: For 'deployed-site' targets: the URL where the finding was observed. Null otherwise.

diff_or_snippet.description: Unified-diff or code-snippet showing the recommended fix. Free-form text — consumers render however they want.

rotated_secrets_needed.description: If the fix requires rotating credentials, list the credential identifiers. Lets /lictor-rotate or any rotation tool auto-generate the runbook.

estimated_effort_minutes.description: Optional rough estimate of fix effort in minutes. Helpful for prioritization in dashboards.

agent.description: For AI-driven tools: which sub-agent / rule / check found this. Lictor uses agent names (radar, sieve, probe, etc.); rule-based scanners use rule IDs.

agent_confidence.description: For AI-driven tools: confidence (0.0–1.0) that the finding is real. Rule-based scanners can omit this. Consumers can use this to threshold low-confidence findings out of default views.

references.description: URLs to documentation, CVE entries, blog posts, or other relevant context for this finding.

cwe.description: Optional CWE (Common Weakness Enumeration) IDs that classify this finding.

cve.description: Optional CVE IDs for findings tied to known published vulnerabilities (typically dependency findings).

fingerprint.description: Optional stable hash of (file_path + canonicalized code_snippet + category) usable for dedup across tools. Format is tool-specific but should be deterministic across runs. (Stable cross-tool fingerprinting is an open question for v0.2.)

agent_attributions.description: For AI-driven tools: per-agent contribution stats. Optional for rule-based scanners.

found_count.description: Number of findings this agent generated.

scored_count.description: Number of findings this agent scored / reviewed (without originating).

confidence_avg.description: Mean agent_confidence across findings this agent generated. Null if agent doesn't generate findings (e.g., orchestrator agents).

notes.description: Free-text annotations from the tool. Use for context the schema doesn't capture (e.g., 'audit ran with --skip-rls-check', 'OpenAI API rate-limited during scan').

findings.title: Supabase service key exposed in client JS bundle
findings.summary: The SUPABASE_SERVICE_ROLE_KEY is bundled into src/lib/db.ts and shipped to every visitor's browser. Anyone with the URL has full database write access.

fix.summary: Move to a server-only route, never bundle in client
fix.diff_or_snippet: // Move db.ts to app/api/_internal/db.ts so Next.js bundler keeps it server-only.
